critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of building a simple Unix shell called 'andsh' in C, covering features like command execution, environment variable expansion, piping, and basic line editing with history and tab completion. It provides code snippets and explanations of key shell concepts such as process forking, execvp, and pipe management.
mainPoints: The author builds a toy shell named 'andsh' to better understand shell internals.
Basic shell features implemented include running commands, built-in 'cd', environment variable expansion, and simple piping.
The shell uses Unix system calls like fork, execvp, waitpid, pipe, and dup2 to manage processes and inter-process communication.
Line editing, history, and tab completion are added using the readline library.
The shell is intentionally minimal and incomplete, lacking quoting, redirection, and advanced builtins.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the process of building a simple Unix shell and correctly explains the use of system calls like fork, execvp, waitpid, pipe, and dup2, which are standard for shell implementations. The code snippets and explanations align with common shell programming practices in C. The author's statements about limitations and missing features are reasonable and transparent. There are no factual inaccuracies detected in the technical content.
considerations: The article does not cover security implications of shell design, such as input sanitization or handling malicious commands.
It omits discussion of more advanced shell features like job control, signal handling beyond basic interrupts, and scripting capabilities.
Performance considerations beyond basic system call overhead are not deeply analyzed.
The article assumes familiarity with C programming and Unix system calls, which may limit accessibility for beginners.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and learning experiences involved in building a shell, highlighting complexities like string parsing, job control, and process management. Several commenters share personal projects and resources, emphasizing the educational value despite the difficulties.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The pipe section is the part that changes how you think about processes. Once you've manually done the dup2 dance — close write-end in parent, close read-end in child, wire them up — it stops being magic and starts being obvious why `grep | sort | uniq` works at all. The thing that surprised me building a similar toy was how late in the process job control has to come: you can get a working pipe chain surprisingly fast, and then job control (SIGTSTP, tcsetpgrp, the whole mess) costs 5x more than everything else combined.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed insight into the technical challenges of implementing pipes and job control, reflecting accurate and nuanced understanding of shell internals.

comments.author: rrampage
comments.text:
Fun read! I built a minimal Linux shell [0] in c and Zig last year which does not depend on libc. It was a great way to learn about execve, the new-ish clone3 syscall and how Linux starts a process. Parsing strings is the least fun part of the building the shell.

[0] https://gist.github.com/rrampage/5046b60ca2d040bcffb49ee38e8...
comments.analysis: The comment shares firsthand experience building a shell with low-level syscalls, confirming common difficulties and learning outcomes, making it a credible and useful contribution.

comments.author: lvales
comments.text:
Building a shell is a great exercise, but honestly having to deal with string parsing is such a bother that it robs like 2/3 of the joy along the way. I once built a very simple one in Go [0] as a learning exercise and I stopped once I started getting frustrated with all the corner cases.

[0] https://github.com/lourencovales/codecrafters/blob/master/sh...
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the frustration many face with string parsing in shell development, providing a relatable perspective supported by personal experience.

comments.author: hexer303
comments.text: Unix shells are conceptually simple but hide a surprising amount of complexity under the hood that we take for granted. I recently had build my own PTY controller. There were so many edge-cases to deal with. It took weeks of stress testing and writing many tests to get it right.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out the hidden complexities of shell components like PTY controllers, underscoring the depth of effort required for robust implementations.

comments.author: emersion
comments.text: Some time ago I've written an article about a particular aspect of shells, job control: https://emersion.fr/blog/2019/job-control/
comments.analysis: Sharing a relevant resource on job control adds value to the discussion, showing expertise and providing further reading for interested participants.

comments.author: mzs
comments.text:
Had an assignment to build a shell in a week, how hard could it be?

  controlling terminal
  session leader
  job control
The parser was easy in comparison.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects the common underestimation of shell complexity, emphasizing that job control and terminal management are more challenging than parsing.

comments.author: ratzkewatzke
comments.text: There's a very good exercise on Codecrafters (https://app.codecrafters.io/courses/shell/overview) to walk you through writing your own shell. I found it enlightening, as well as a good way to learn a new language.
comments.analysis: Recommending a structured learning resource is helpful and likely accurate, supporting newcomers interested in shell development.

comments.author: doe88
comments.text: Is there a (real) shell whose code is relatively short and self contained and would be valuable to read? This was always something I wanted to do but never quite spent time to look for a good one to explore.
comments.analysis: This question is constructive and relevant, seeking practical advice for learning, though it does not provide factual information itself.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: Bit of pedantry but I don't think traditional unix shell (like this) follows repl model; the shell is not usually doing printing of the result of evaluation. Instead the printing happens more as a side effect of the commands.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a precise technical clarification about shell behavior, which is accurate and adds nuance to the discussion.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: Interesting. I wanted to do toast | bash to let the AI drive the computer but the bash shell really got in the way. Too much complexity. The things that annoy humans, $ expansion, special characters, etc don't work for AI either. Ended up writing a custom shell for AI (and humans). When a tool gets in the way, sometimes it just time to change the tool.
comments.analysis: While the comment shares an interesting perspective on shell complexity interfering with AI use, it is more anecdotal and speculative.

comments.author: lasgawe
comments.text: Great article. There are many things every developer should do when starting to learn programming or when trying to improve their skills. This is one of them. I once built a shell-like programming language (not an interpreter). If anyone reading this wants to improve their skills, I strongly suggest building your own shell from scratch.
comments.analysis: This comment is encouraging and motivational but lacks technical depth or critical analysis.

comments.author: austy69
comments.text: Fun read. Wonder if you are able to edit text in the shell, or if you need to implement a gap buffer to allow it?
comments.analysis: The comment raises a relevant question about shell input editing but does not provide factual information or analysis.

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: Link was previously posted by author: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47398749 There are other good quality articles on their site, and maybe deserves the imaginary points.
comments.analysis: This comment adds little value to the discussion, merely noting link reposting without substantive content.
